name: Anaphylaxis
body:
**Anaphylaxis** is a severe, life-threatening systemic hypersensitivity reaction with rapid onset following exposure to an allergen (foods, drugs, Hymenoptera venom, latex). The diagnosis is clinical: mucocutaneous involvement (urticaria, angioedema) plus respiratory (stridor, wheezing, dyspnea), cardiovascular (hypotension, syncope), or severe gastrointestinal involvement following probable exposure.

The immediate treatment is **intramuscular adrenaline** (anterolateral aspect of the thigh, dose based on weight; repeat every 5–15 min if the condition persists). Place the patient in a supine position with legs elevated, unless severe dyspnea is present (semi-seated). Administer oxygen, fluid expansion with crystalloids if hypotension exists, and **H1 + H2 antihistamines** and corticosteroids as adjuncts (these do not replace adrenaline). Remove the triggering agent if it is an IV drug. Observe for at least 4–6 hours (risk of biphasic reaction); discharge with an adrenaline autoinjector if appropriate.

**Nursing:** identify and remove the allergen, call for help, record the exact time of adrenaline administration, monitor BP, HR, and SpO₂, prepare a second dose and advanced airway. Document the allergen and alert in the medical record.

clinicalBox.title: Adrenaline IM — Orientative Dosage
clinicalBox.content:
Intramuscular route (anterolateral thigh), concentration 1:1000 (1 mg/mL):
**Adult:** 0.3–0.5 mg IM (0.3–0.5 mL)
**Child:** 0.01 mg/kg IM (max. 0.3 mg)

Repeat every **5–15 min** if there is no clinical improvement.
In cardiorespiratory arrest: IV adrenaline according to ACLS / institutional pediatric protocol.

bibliography.citation: Sociedad Argentina de Alergia e Inmunología (SAAI). Consenso de anafilaxia.
Ministerio de Salud de la Nación Argentina. Estrategias y guías de atención.
National Institute for Allergy and Infectious Diseases. Anaphylaxis guidelines.
